Hey, you're back here for the next lesson here. Now we're moving into the actual nitty gritty, which is language patterns. Now just to be clear here, I want you to really understand that the back end of the system and the sort of after you're done with each lesson, the quizzes, this is an area where this particularly important because there's going to be exercises that show you that take you step by step and help you construct the perfect straight line script based on which part of the script you're at. So there'll be sort of these breakdowns for you based on which lesson we're at right now. We'll show you how to actually construct your script. So please, please, please make sure you spend extra time in the quizzes right now to make sure you construct your script as we go along. All right. Let me start off by saying that when we talk about language patterns. This is what I mean when I say that basically as you move down the straight line from the open to the close, every word you say, everything you do, every document you hand the prospect, every sales aid use, every tonality you apply, every bit of body language, it's designed to essentially increase their level of certainty for one, two, three of the three tens, right? That's what you're doing is you move someone down the straight line. You're using language patterns along with the tonality and body language that comes with that to essentially create massive certainty in the heart and mind of your prospect. Now when I say heart and mind, those are basically co-words for logical certainty and emotional certainty. Now while logical certainty is a direct result of the words that you say, and while emotional certainty is more about future pacing, remember you future pace by using words. So even though we say, talk about words for logic, you're using words for emotion too, but when it comes to emotion, it's more about the picture that the words paint than the actual logical case made by the ABCD of words. Does that make sense? All right, now remember in the syntax of the straight line, we always create logic first and emotion second because when you create that airtight logical case, it then frees the prospect up to be moved emotionally, right? So with that, now let's dive right in. We'll start with the introduction of the opening here. And there are six basic points I want to reinforce here. I have to do with that opening. So the opening is when you basically start off by introducing yourself and your company. You say a couple of short things for the reason of your call and it goes right up to the point when you ask permission to start asking questions, that's your next jump, right? So let me give you the six important points here. Number one, you want to be enthusiastic right from the start. You don't sort of say, hi, Mr. Jones. You got to come out there right out of the gate. You want to be sharp as a tack, enthusiastic as hell, an expert in your field. It starts right out of the gate. There's a certain energy that comes out of you. And I've been through this with tonality and violence. You get this by now, right? But just remember you want to be enthusiastic right from the start. Number two, always speak in the familiar. Now this is crucial. You never would say, hello, is Mr. Jones there? Say, hey, is Bill there? You get it? So it's not, hello, is Mr. Jones there? That's like, eh, salesman alert. That's not how no one will speak. You say, hey, is Mr. Jones there? No, it's, hey, is Bill there? That's your way of doing it. Okay, so you always want to speak in the familiar words. Okay, number three, you start off by introducing yourself and your company. And then you want to restate the company's name one more time right in the opening after you save the reason for your call. So watch, it's be like, you know, hey, Mr. Jones, Jordan Belfort calling from XYZ company. How you doing today? He says, okay, say, great. Now, Jim, the reason for the call is you might have heard of XYZ company and you say the company name and that's your basic introduction. And again, I just want to kind of show you right now here in this lesson, but in the back end in your quiz, I'm going to actually make you write this stuff out, give you a template to really lock it in. Okay, it's easier that way. It's going to give you the generalities now, right? Next, number four, you want to use what's called power words dramatically, fastest growing. Actually, certain words, just for whatever reason, they just sound good. They enhance what you're doing and they give the prospect the sense that, you know, this is something you want to listen to, it's important and it just enhances your ability to capture their attention and get them engaged. All right, now moving on to number five, you always want to ask for permission to ask questions. And of course, I went through this in detail in the prospecting section. So I'm not going to beat a dead horse again. But just remember here, if you don't ask for permission, you're going to break rapport, you're going to come up with that awful grand inquisitor who just wants to dig, dig, dig versus the trusted friend, the family physician, the person that really has their best interest at heart. And then number six, you'll always want to use that all important justifier, that's so. So you would say, just a couple of questions so I don't waste your time. So I can best serve you. And again, that so is your justifier. And it dramatically increases the compliance, right? As we spoke about in prospecting, right? So by doing that, you ensure that when you transition to this next section, when we ask in questions, you're positioned in the perfect way, which is as the trusted friend, the family advisor, the doctor, whatever you want to say, right? That really has that best interest at heart and is asking questions because you care. Now, to that end, as far as the actual intelligence gathering questions go, we've been through that in prospecting ad nauseam. So I'm not going to get into that right now, but in the back end, in the resource center, with your quizzes, remember, we're paying special attention to this particular section because I'm going to go through and help you actually design your questions for your script that follows every rule that we laid out in straight line prospecting. Make sense? Okay, great. Now, remember, the last part of prospecting is what? Your transition. So this is the point now at the end of those questions. And this is when you actually get to separate out the buyers or heat and buyers in power. You move them down the straight line and you're going to eliminate the look you lose in the mistakes, right? So that's one part. And for those that make it down the street on those first two groups, right? We're going to say, you know, Jim, Jill, John, Juan, Sam, whatever it is, you know, based on what you just said to me, this is a perfect fit for you. Let me tell you why. Simple as that. I'll say it again, right? John, Jill, John, based on everything you just said to me, now watch, based on everything you just said to me, right? So we're tailoring it to them. We're saying that based on the questions that you asked, based on that, what I have here, my product, my program, my idea, whatever it might be, this particular TV, it doesn't could be anything tangible, intangible. Doesn't matter. Is a perfect fit for you. Let me tell you why. And from there now, you're going to transition to the main body of your straight line presentation.
